explanation: This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F), the robot will provide an explanation (explain) that includes both how and why the failure occurred (diagnose & provideReason). The use of F guarantees that the explanation will eventually be given. The use of & ensures that both explanation requirements are met. This directly addresses the prompt's requirement. No other LTL property is needed.

The LTL property ensures that if a failure is detected (represented by the atomic proposition 'failureDetected'), then eventually the robot provides an explanation. This explanation should be both detailed ('detailed') and include the reasons for the failure ('provideReason').

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot will explain how and why the failure occurred (explain & diagnose). It captures the requirement by ensuring that the robot provides an explanation and diagnosis following a failure.  Alternative formulations that focus solely on 'explain' may miss the critical need for the robot to also diagnose the issue.  Formulations that focus on 'diagnose' alone would miss the request for an explanation.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot should provide an explanation (explain) and the reason behind the failure (provideReason). This directly addresses the prompt by ensuring the robot explains how and why when something fails. Previous LTL properties were not provided, so their shortcomings are not assessed.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected, then eventually, the robot should provide an explanation that includes the cause and the reason for the failure. This captures the requirement that the robot should explain why it failed in a given situation. Other options are incorrect because they do not capture the 'how' and 'why' components.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected, eventually the robot will provide an explanation. The explanation must include the how and why of the failure. The property uses the 'F' (eventually) operator to capture the requirement that the explanation must happen at some point after the failure. The use of 'provideReason' is included because providing an explanation inherently contains the 'why' aspect. The use of 'explain' covers the 'how'.

The LTL property expresses that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot will explain why the failure occurred (explain) and provide details (detailed). This captures the requirement that the robot should explain how and why in the event of a failure. Previous properties were not provided.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot will provide an explanation (explain) that is detailed (detailed). This captures the requirement that upon failure, the robot must explain 'how and why'. The use of 'eventually' (F) ensures that the explanation happens at some point after the failure. The property does not include any numerical comparisons as requested.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot should provide an explanation (explain) and the reason why the failure occurred (provideReason). This captures the 'how and why' requirement of the natural language input.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected, then eventually the robot will provide an explanation. The property captures the natural language requirement by ensuring that if a problem occurs (failureDetected), the robot will eventually explain how and why the failure occurred, aligning with the natural language prompt.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot will provide an explanation (explain) that is detailed (detailed) and also provides a reason (provideReason). This captures the essence of the prompt: explaining the 'how and why' when something fails. There are no previous LTL properties.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot will provide an explanation (explain) of the failure, including how the failure occurred (diagnose). This captures the prompt's requirement that the robot explain how and why it failed.

The LTL property `G(failureDetected -> (explain & provideReason))` states that globally, if a failure is detected, then it should explain how and why. This captures the requirement that the robot should provide an explanation when a failure occurs. The explanation is supposed to include how the robot failed and the reason for the failure.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot will explain how and why (explain). It directly addresses the prompt's requirement that the robot explain the cause of failures.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (problemDetected), then eventually (F) the robot must explain the failure (explain) and provide a reason for the failure (provideReason). This directly captures the essence of the input natural language.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot will explain how (explain) and why (provideReason). The LTL property directly translates the natural language prompt. The use of 'F' (eventually) ensures that the explanation happens at some point after the failure is detected.  Earlier attempts may have missed the crucial requirement for both 'how' and 'why' which is now covered by the combination of 'explain' and 'provideReason'.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot should both explain (explain) how and why (provideReason). This captures the prompt's requirement that the robot should explain how and why if something fails. The use of 'F' ensures that the explanation is provided at some point after the failure. The use of '&' makes sure that both conditions apply.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot will provide an explanation (explain) along with the reason for the failure (provideReason). This captures the essence of the prompt, which requires the robot to explain how and why it failed.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot must explain why the failure occurred (explain) and provide a diagnosis (diagnose). This captures the requirement that the robot must explain how and why it failed, ensuring the system provides detailed information about the failure. Previous LTL properties were not provided, so there is nothing to compare to.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot will explain how and why (explain & provideReason).  This directly captures the natural language requirement.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected, then eventually, the robot should explain and provide a reason. This directly addresses the prompt's requirement that the robot explains how and why in the event of a failure. The 'F' (eventually) ensures that the explanation occurs at some point after the failure. Previously given LTL properties would not fully capture the natural language prompt as the explanation and the reason behind the explanation is critical to fulfil the prompt.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot will provide an explanation (explain) of the failure, and also provide a reason (provideReason). This directly addresses the prompt's requirement for explanation of the failure.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot provides an explanation (explain) and provides a reason (provideReason). This captures the requirement that the robot should explain how and why something failed. The property does not need to account for any other conditions. Other potential LTL properties would add extra conditions and would therefore not precisely capture the prompt.

The LTL property expresses that if a failure is detected (problemDetected), then eventually the robot will provide an explanation (explain) of the failure and provide a reason (provideReason) why the failure occurred. The property uses the 'F' (eventually) operator to ensure the explanation and reason are provided at some point in the future after the failure. This translation directly addresses the prompt's requirement for an explanation of the failure, and how and why.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected, then eventually the robot will explain the failure and provide a reason. The use of 'F' (eventually) ensures that the explanation and the reason are provided at some point after the failure detection.  The absence of 'G' (globally) implies that this explanation is not required indefinitely, just in response to a failure.  The property correctly captures the requirement that an explanation, including why the failure occurred, must be given after a failure is detected. Other potential solutions may be less precise and fail to capture the requirement that both the explanation and the reason are provided.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F), the robot will explain (explain) how and why (provideReason) the failure occurred. This directly captures the requirement that the robot should provide an explanation upon failure.

The LTL formula ensures that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot will provide an explanation (explain) including how and why the failure occurred (provideReason & detailRequested). This captures the prompt's requirement for the robot to explain failures.  The use of 'eventually' (F) is appropriate because the explanation does not need to happen immediately, only sometime after the failure. The 'provideReason' and 'detailRequested' ensure the explanation is complete, covering both the cause and the context of the failure, as requested by the prompt.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot will provide an explanation (explain) that is detailed (detailed). This captures the essence of the natural language prompt by ensuring that upon failure, the robot not only explains the issue but also provides a comprehensive explanation. No prior LTL properties were given.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected, then eventually, the robot must provide an explanation. The explanation must be detailed, and also include the reason for the failure. This accurately captures the original requirement.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot should explain the reason (provideReason) and the 'how' which is captured implicitly here, i.e. by providing an explanation. This directly addresses the prompt's requirement for the robot to explain how and why in the event of a failure.  There is no need to incorporate the concept of time here, so we don't use the 'X' operator. Also, all the atomic propositions were defined and relevant to the current scenario.

This LTL property specifies that if a failure is detected, then eventually the robot should explain how and why the failure occurred. It uses the 'F' (eventually) operator to express this temporal relationship. The atomic propositions used directly map to the concepts in the prompt, ensuring all requirements are captured. This property directly addresses the prompt by mandating an explanation following a failure. No other LTL properties are applicable as no additional information or constraints is present.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot will explain (explain) the failure and provide a reason (provideReason). This property captures the requirement that the robot should explain how and why it failed. Previous LTL properties are not applicable because they do not capture the essence of explaining how and why the robot has failed.
This LTL property ensures that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), the robot will eventually provide an explanation (explain) that includes the reason for the failure (provideReason).

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected, then eventually the robot must explain why and how. This property directly addresses the prompt by ensuring that when a failure occurs, the robot provides an explanation. There are no previous LTL properties.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected, then eventually the robot will provide an explanation that is both detailed and provides a reason (why) for the failure. This captures the essence of the natural language prompt, which asks for an explanation of the failure, including the 'how and why'.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F), the robot should explain (explain) and provide a reason (provideReason). This directly captures the prompt's requirement for explanation upon failure.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot must explain how (explain) and why (provideReason) the failure occurred. This aligns directly with the input natural language request. The property uses a simple implication structure to ensure that the explanation is provided in the future if the failure is detected.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected, eventually the robot will both explain and diagnose.  This translation captures the essence of the prompt by ensuring the robot explains the "how" and "why" (diagnosis) of the failure.  Previous LTL properties were not provided.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected, then eventually the robot will explain the failure. The explanation must be detailed (detailed & explain). Previously given LTLs may not fully capture the prompt as they might not require a detailed explanation or explain how and why.

The LTL property expresses that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot must explain how and why the failure occurred (explain & provideReason). This directly captures the requirement of the natural language prompt. There are no prior LTL properties in the context, so we do not consider them in this explanation.
This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F), the robot must provide an explanation (explain), and diagnose the issue (diagnose).

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot will explain why (provideReason). This captures the essence of the input, requiring the robot to provide an explanation upon failure.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot will explain how and why the failure happened. The 'G(failureDetected -> F (explain & provideReason))' ensures that after a failure is detected, the robot must eventually explain both the 'how' and the 'why', capturing the essence of the natural language input.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected, then eventually the robot should explain the reason and how the failure occurred. This directly addresses the prompt by ensuring the robot provides an explanation in case of failure. There is no previous LTL property to compare to.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot will provide an explanation (explain) and the robot will diagnose the failure (diagnose). The property captures the essence of the natural language requirement by ensuring that in the event of failure, the robot will explain the situation and diagnose what went wrong. Previous properties were not provided, so they are not addressed.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (represented by 'failureDetected'), then eventually the robot will provide an explanation (represented by 'explain') and also provide a reason (represented by 'provideReason'). This captures the prompt's requirement that the robot explains how and why after something fails.

The LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot will provide an explanation (explain) that is detailed (detailed) and provides the reason for the failure (provideReason). This directly reflects the prompt.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually the robot should provide an explanation (explain) and provide a reason (provideReason). This captures the essence of the natural language input.  Previous LTL properties were not provided to compare against.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F) the robot should provide an explanation (explain) along with the diagnosis (diagnose) regarding that failure. It captures the prompt by specifying the reaction to a failure should involve an explanation and diagnostic analysis.

This LTL property states that if a failure is detected (failureDetected), then eventually (F), the robot should provide an explanation (explain) and provide a reason (provideReason). This directly captures the requirement of explaining why and how after a failure.
